title: CBA offers staff $3000 a head to recommend private bank recruits
full_text:
Commonwealth Bank is offering cash bonuses to staff who help recruit top talent for its private bank - the division that works with its wealthiest clients - as the major lenders continue to compete hard for experienced employees.
Staff in the private bank will get $3000 per referral if the person they recommend for a job is hired. The offer has been in place since April and will run until June 30.
The private bank works with households that earn more than $450,000 a year and plan to invest or borrow at least $2.5 million.
Last year, CBA unveiled a new wealth platform to drive growth in the private division, which suggests asset allocations, model portfolios and investment products based on a customer's risk appetite.
"As do many organisations, CBA uses a range of recruitment and referral approaches based on business needs and the skills required," a CBA spokesman said.
CBA is known to use cash incentives to attract staff. Last March, it was on a drive to poach the industry's top mortgage brokers with the lure of bonus-heavy pay packets.
Offers to brokers included a base salary of up to $150,000, with take-home pay potentially rising to $270,000 if all bonus hurdles were cleared.
The bank was also the first to controversially remove caps on bonuses for its best-performing home lenders - a condition that had been agreed to by the banks after the damaging Hayne banking royal commission.
Amid intensifying mortgage competition in 2024, CBA lifted the maximum bonus it can pay its best home loan bankers from 50 per cent of base pay - the limit imposed by a Stephen Sedgwick-led review for the Australian Banking Association in 2017 - to 80 per cent of base pay.
April's recruitment drive came as Westpac and National Australia Bank raided talent across multiple divisions.
Most recently, Westpac hired Rebecca James to be the managing director of its commercial banking division. James was a group executive at CBA's New Zealand subsidiary, ASB.
James joins half a dozen senior leaders Westpac has poached from CBA, including Paul Fowler to lead the business bank and Andrew McMullan, who was hired last year as Westpac's chief data, digital and AI officer.
In institutional banking, Westpac's head of division Nell Hutton poached Carli Renzi to be the head of financial institutions and government. She was a managing director in CBA's institutional bank and was previously at ANZ.
Alex Toone defected to Westpac after 13 years running CBA's commodities team, while David Watts left CBA to run data and AI for Hutton.
Meanwhile, NAB has poached CBA's Mahya Knox to become its new chief AI officer.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking and Economy
